There is no better proof of ignorance than a sense of knowing everything. Much can be known even when nothing is known to good purpose. And those who think they know something and become proud because of it are the least likely to make good use of what they know. Satan harms some as much by tempting them to be proud of their mental abilities, as he does others by luring them to sensuality. Knowledge that makes a person arrogant and confident is as dangerous as self-righteous pride, even if what they know is correct. Without genuine faith, all human knowledge is worthless. The pagans had gods of higher and lower degree; many gods, and many lords; so called, but not such in truth. Christians know better. One God made everything and has power over everything. This one God, even the Father, signifies Godhead as the sole object of all religious worship; and the Lord Jesus Christ denotes the person of Emmanuel, God manifest in the flesh, One with the Father, and with us; the appointed Mediator, and Lord of all; through whom we come to the Father, and through whom the Father sends all blessings to us, by the influence and working of the Holy Spirit. While we refuse worship to those who are called gods and lords, and to saints and angels, let us examine whether we truly come to God by faith in Christ.

Eating one kind of food and avoiding another has nothing that recommends a person to God. But the apostle cautions against putting a stumbling block in the way of the weak; lest they be made bold to eat what was offered to an idol, not as common food, but as a sacrifice, and thereby be guilty of idolatry. Whoever has the Spirit of Christ in him will love those whom Christ loved so much that he died for them. Injuries done to Christians are done to Christ; especially when it entangles them in guilt: wounding their consciences wounds him. We should be very careful about doing anything that may cause others to stumble, even if it is innocent in itself. And if we must not endanger other men's souls, how much more should we take care not to destroy our own! Let Christians beware of approaching the edge of evil, or the appearance of it, though many do this in public matters, for which they perhaps plead convincingly. People cannot sin against their brothers and sisters in this way without offending Christ and endangering their own souls.
